title: LoRA/QLoRA Fine-Tuning Pipeline with PEFT
description: Complete step-by-step workflow for fine-tuning an LLM using LoRA or QLoRA with HuggingFace PEFT. Covers configuration of LoraConfig, selecting target modules (all linear layers recommended), training with SFTTrainer, merging adapters back into the base model, and deploying the merged model. This is the foundational workflow that 90% of production fine-tuning jobs follow.
workflow: Install dependencies: pip install transformers peft trl datasets bitsandbytes accelerate
Load a base model and tokenizer with AutoModelForCausalLM and AutoTokenizer
Configure LoRA with LoraConfig (r=16, lora_alpha=32, target_modules=['q_proj','k_proj','v_proj','o_proj','gate_proj','up_proj','down_proj'], task_type='CAUSAL_LM')
For QLoRA: add BitsAndBytesConfig (load_in_4bit=True, bnb_4bit_quant_type='nf4', bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16)
Wrap model with get_peft_model(model, lora_config) and print trainable parameters
Prepare dataset in chat format using tokenizer.apply_chat_template()
Configure SFTConfig with learning_rate=2e-4 (LoRA) or 2e-5 (QLoRA), per_device_train_batch_size=4, gradient_accumulation_steps=4, bf16=True, gradient_checkpointing=True
Train with SFTTrainer and monitor loss, reward margins, and token accuracy
Save adapter with trainer.save_model()
Merge adapter into base model with model.merge_and_unload() for deployment

title: QLoRA Fine-Tuning with PEFT and BitsAndBytes
description: Working Python code for 4-bit QLoRA fine-tuning of a 7B parameter model on a single consumer GPU. Demonstrates the BitsAndBytes quantization config, LoRA adapter setup with all linear projections as targets, and training with SFTTrainer. The 4-bit quantization reduces VRAM from ~16GB (LoRA) to ~6GB (QLoRA) for 7B models, enabling fine-tuning on free Colab T4 GPUs.
code:
import torch
from datasets import load_dataset
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, prepare_model_for_kbit_training
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig, TrainingArguments
from trl import SFTTrainer, SFTConfig

model_id = 'meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct'

# 4-bit quantization config
bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type='nf4',
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
)

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id,
    quantization_config=bnb_config,
    device_map='auto',
    attn_implementation='flash_attention_2',
)
model = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(model)

# LoRA config targeting all linear projections
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=16,
    lora_alpha=32,
    target_modules=['q_proj', 'k_proj', 'v_proj', 'o_proj', 'gate_proj', 'up_proj', 'down_proj'],
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias='none',
    task_type='CAUSAL_LM',
)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()

# Load and format dataset
dataset = load_dataset('trl-lib/Capybara', split='train')

tokenizer.chat_template = None  # use model's default

targs = SFTConfig(
    output_dir='./qlora-output',
    num_train_epochs=3,
    per_device_train_batch_size=2,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,
    learning_rate=2e-5,
    bf16=True,
    logging_steps=10,
    gradient_checkpointing=True,
    optim='paged_adamw_8bit',
    save_strategy='epoch',
    max_seq_length=2048,
)

trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=targs,
    train_dataset=dataset,
    processing_class=tokenizer,
)
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model('./qlora-adapter')

title: Fine-Tuning LLMs with LoRA and QLoRA: A Practical Deep-Dive
description: An extensive 2026 deep-dive covering the memory wall problem, LoRA math (delta-W = B x A low-rank decomposition), QLoRA innovations (NF4 quantization, double quantization, paged optimizers), complete runnable training and merge/inference scripts for Mistral-7B-Instruct, performance comparison tables across model sizes, evaluation strategy with MMLU canary, and seven common failure modes with fixes.

title: LoRA and QLoRA Fine-Tuning Guide 2026 (AI Workflow Lab)
description: A production-oriented 2026 guide covering when fine-tuning beats prompting, LoRA and QLoRA implementation with Llama 3.1-8B-Instruct, evaluation framework with automated pass-rate testing, merging and GGUF conversion for Ollama deployment, DoRA (rank-stabilized LoRA), and hardware requirements table. Includes decision guide for choosing between LoRA, QLoRA, Full FT, and DPO.

title: LoRA Adapter Merging and Export to GGUF
description: Working Python code for merging a trained LoRA adapter back into the base model, saving the merged model in fp16 safetensors format, converting to GGUF using llama.cpp, and quantizing to Q4_K_M for efficient local deployment. This is the standard deployment pipeline for LoRA-fine-tuned models destined for Ollama or llama.cpp inference.
code:
from peft import PeftModel
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
import subprocess
import os

base_model_id = 'meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct'
adapter_path = './qlora-adapter'
merged_path = './merged-model'
gguf_path = './llama-3.1-8b-finetuned'

# Step 1: Load base model and merge adapter
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(base_model_id)
base_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    base_model_id,
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    device_map='cpu',  # merge on CPU to avoid VRAM issues
)
model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base_model, adapter_path)
model = model.merge_and_unload()

# Step 2: Save merged model in safetensors format
model.save_pretrained(merged_path, safe_serialization=True)
tokenizer.save_pretrained(merged_path)
print(f'Merged model saved to {merged_path}')

# Step 3: Convert to GGUF F16 format
subprocess.run([
    'python', 'llama.cpp/convert_hf_to_gguf.py',
    merged_path,
    '--outfile', f'{gguf_path}-f16.gguf',
    '--outtype', 'f16',
], check=True)

# Step 4: Quantize to Q4_K_M for deployment
subprocess.run([
    './llama.cpp/llama-quantize',
    f'{gguf_path}-f16.gguf',
    f'{gguf_path}-Q4_K_M.gguf',
    'Q4_K_M',
], check=True)

print(f'GGUF model ready: {gguf_path}-Q4_K_M.gguf')
print('Deploy with: ollama create my-model -f Modelfile')

title: Unsloth: 2-5x Faster LLM Fine-Tuning
description: Unsloth provides hand-optimized CUDA/Triton kernels for LLM fine-tuning, achieving 2-5x speedup and up to 70% less VRAM compared to vanilla HuggingFace PEFT. Supports 500+ models including Llama 4, Qwen3, DeepSeek, and Gemma 4. Features include GRPO reinforcement learning, MoE fine-tuning (12x faster), vision/audio/multimodal support, and one-click GGUF export. Unsloth Studio provides a web UI for non-developers.

title: Axolotl: Configurable LLM Fine-Tuning Framework
description: Axolotl is a YAML-driven fine-tuning framework supporting the widest range of models and training methods. Features include DeepSpeed ZeRO 1/2/3 for multi-GPU, SonicMoE/ScatterMoE fused LoRA for MoE models, ND Parallelism (context + tensor + FSDP), SageAttention, GDPO, reward modeling, FP8 fine-tuning via torchao, and multimodal training (vision, audio). Now uses uv-first installation and includes AI agent documentation for Claude Code, Cursor, and Copilot.

title: torchtune: PyTorch Native LLM Fine-Tuning
description: torchtune is Meta's official PyTorch library for LLM fine-tuning, providing composable recipes (standalone Python scripts) for SFT, LoRA/QLoRA, DPO, PPO, and knowledge distillation. Supports Llama 4, Qwen3, Gemma2, Phi4, and Mistral. Best Apple Silicon (MPS) support among fine-tuning frameworks. Memory-efficient QLoRA achieves 81.9% less memory than baseline with 284% speed increase. Uses FSDP2 for distributed training.

title: How to Create Data for Fine-Tuning LLMs (DigitalOcean)
description: End-to-end data preparation guide covering three main formats (completion-style, instruction-style/Alpaca, chat-style/OpenAI messages), code examples for formatting HuggingFace datasets (Dolly, OpenOrca), synthetic data generation using local LLMs without paid APIs, web scraping pipelines for domain-specific data, and the key principle that quality matters more than quantity.

title: LLM Fine-Tuning Dataset Preparation: End-to-End Guide
description: Comprehensive guide covering SFT, DPO/reward model, and preference learning data formats with detailed schemas. Includes deduplication (exact + MinHash/SimHash), PII redaction, safety taxonomy, split leakage prevention, and a QA checklist. Recommends 80k SFT items as baseline: 60% general instructions, 25% domain tasks, 15% policy/safety.

title: Dataset Preparation and Formatting for SFT
description: Working Python code for preparing a dataset in the three standard formats (Alpaca instruction, chat messages, and prompt-completion), applying chat templates, splitting into train/validation/test, and pushing to HuggingFace Hub. Demonstrates the most common data preparation patterns used in production fine-tuning pipelines.
code:
from datasets import load_dataset, DatasetDict
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

# Load raw dataset
raw = load_dataset('HuggingFaceH4/no_robots', split='train')
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct')

# Format 1: Alpaca-style instruction format
def format_alpaca(example):
    return {
        'instruction': example['instruction'],
        'input': example.get('input', ''),
        'output': example['response'],
    }

alpaca_data = raw.map(format_alpaca)

# Format 2: Chat/messages format (OpenAI-style)
def format_messages(example):
    messages = []
    if example.get('system_prompt'):
        messages.append({'role': 'system', 'content': example['system_prompt']})
    messages.append({'role': 'user', 'content': example['instruction']})
    messages.append({'role': 'assistant', 'content': example['response']})
    return {
        'messages': messages,
        'text': tokenizer.apply_chat_template(messages, tokenize=False),
    }

chat_data = raw.map(format_messages)

# Format 3: Prompt-completion format
def format_prompt_completion(example):
    prompt = example['instruction']
    if example.get('input'):
        prompt += f'\n\n{example["input"]}'
    return {
        'prompt': prompt,
        'completion': example['response'],
    }

pc_data = raw.map(format_prompt_completion)

# Split into train/val/test (90/5/5)
split = chat_data.train_test_split(test_size=0.1, seed=42)
test_val = split['test'].train_test_split(test_size=0.5, seed=42)
final_dataset = DatasetDict({
    'train': split['train'],
    'validation': test_val['train'],
    'test': test_val['test'],
})

final_dataset.push_to_hub('my-username/my-sft-dataset')
print(f'Train: {len(final_dataset["train"])} samples')
print(f'Val: {len(final_dataset["validation"])} samples')
print(f'Test: {len(final_dataset["test"])} samples')

title: FSDP Multi-GPU Fine-Tuning Workflow
description: Step-by-step workflow for distributed fine-tuning of large models using PyTorch FSDP2 (Fully Sharded Data Parallel). Covers RAM-efficient model loading (rank-0 only), sharded checkpoints, Flash Attention V2, gradient checkpointing, and mixed precision policies. Enables fine-tuning 70B models on as few as 16 A100-80GB GPUs.
workflow: Install dependencies: pip install torch transformers accelerate datasets trl peft
Configure FSDP2 sharding with fully_shard() on transformer blocks + root model
Set MixedPrecisionPolicy: param_dtype=bfloat16, reduce_dtype=float32 for stable gradient reduction
Load model on rank 0 only; use sync_module_states=True for broadcasting to other ranks
Enable Flash Attention V2 (attn_implementation='flash_attention_2') for memory-efficient attention
Enable gradient checkpointing (gradient_checkpointing=True) to reduce peak VRAM
Use SHARDED_STATE_DICT for intermediate checkpoint saves; FULL_STATE_DICT only for final export
Configure backward prefetch (BACKWARD_PRE) to overlap all-gather with gradient computation
Launch training with torchrun --nproc_per_node=NUM_GPUS train.py
Monitor with torch.distributed.tensor.debug and NVIDIA DCGM metrics

title: HuggingFace TRL SFTTrainer Documentation
description: Official documentation for HuggingFace TRL's SFTTrainer covering all four dataset formats (standard LM, conversational, prompt-completion, conversational prompt-completion), packing, assistant-only loss, PEFT/LoRA integration, instruction tuning, vision-language model training, tool calling support, and loss types (NLL, DFT, chunked NLL). Includes quick-start code and integration guides for Unsloth and Liger Kernel.

title: DPO Alignment Fine-Tuning Workflow
description: Step-by-step workflow for aligning an SFT model using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). DPO is simpler and more stable than PPO-based RLHF because it eliminates the need for a separate reward model. The workflow covers on-policy preference data generation, DPO training with TRL's DPOTrainer, and evaluation with reward margins and benchmark scores.
workflow: Start with a fine-tuned SFT model (not a base model)
Generate on-policy preference data: prompt the SFT model, score outputs with a reward function, create (chosen, rejected) pairs
Format preference dataset with 'prompt', 'chosen', and 'rejected' columns
Configure DPOTrainer with beta=0.1 (alignment strength), learning_rate=5e-6 (10-100x smaller than SFT), and QLoRA for memory efficiency
Train with DPOTrainer, monitoring rewards/chosen, rewards/rejected, and reward margins
Evaluate on held-out preference data and general capability benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K)
Watch for reward hacking: model may learn to game the reward signal rather than genuinely improving
Merge the DPO adapter with the SFT model for deployment

title: TRL DPOTrainer Documentation
description: Official HuggingFace TRL documentation for DPOTrainer covering the DPO loss function (sigmoid, hinge, IPO, KTO, SimPO, and more), multi-loss combinations (MPO), PEFT/LoRA adapter support, Liger Kernel integration for memory efficiency, vision-language model support, tool calling, and detailed metrics logging (rewards/chosen, rewards/rejected, reward margins, accuracies).

title: Guide to RL Post-Training Algorithms: PPO, DPO, GRPO, and Beyond
description: Comprehensive theoretical guide from HuggingFace covering the full RL post-training landscape: REINFORCE, PPO (with GAE, Actor-Critic, PPO-CLIP, PPO-KLPEN), GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization from DeepSeek-R1), DPO (full mathematical derivation from RLHF), Iterative/Online DPO, IPO, KTO (unpaired data via Prospect Theory), and SimPO (reference-model-free). Includes KL divergence deep dive and practical hyperparameter guidance.

title: EleutherAI LM Evaluation Harness
description: The de facto standard framework for benchmarking LLMs with 12,500+ GitHub stars. Supports HuggingFace, vLLM, SGLang, and API backends. Built-in PEFT/LoRA adapter evaluation via peft=PATH argument. Includes 100+ benchmark tasks (MMLU, HellaSwag, ARC, WinoGrande, GSM8K, etc.). 2025 updates include SGLang backend, think-end token stripping for CoT models, and model steering support.

title: Evaluating Fine-Tuned Models with LM Evaluation Harness
description: Step-by-step workflow for benchmarking a fine-tuned model using EleutherAI's LM Evaluation Harness. Covers installing the harness, evaluating with and without LoRA adapters, selecting appropriate benchmarks, interpreting metrics, and detecting catastrophic forgetting by comparing against the base model.
workflow: Install LM Evaluation Harness: pip install lm_eval[hf]
Evaluate the BASE model first as baseline: lm_eval --model hf --model_args pretrained=meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct --tasks mmlu,hellaswag,arc_challenge,gsm8k --device cuda:0

Evaluate your FINE-TUNED model with LoRA adapter: lm_eval --model hf --model_args pretrained=meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct,peft=./qlora-adapter --tasks mmlu,hellaswag,arc_challenge,gsm8k --device cuda:0
Compare scores: if MMLU drops significantly (>2%) while task metrics improve, the model may have catastrophic forgetting
For vLLM backend (faster inference): pip install lm_eval[vllm] then use --model vllm --model_args pretrained=model-name
Use YAML config files for reproducible evaluations: lm-eval run --config my_eval.yaml
Run domain-specific benchmarks alongside general ones to catch trade-offs
Log results to Weights & Biases or MLflow for tracking across training runs

title: LoRA Adapter Merging and GGUF Export for Local Deployment
description: Complete workflow for merging LoRA adapters into the base model, converting to GGUF format, quantizing for efficient local inference, and deploying with Ollama. Covers the critical step of merging before quantization (never quantize before merging), importance matrix quantization for better quality, and Ollama Modelfile creation.
workflow: Train LoRA adapter and save with trainer.save_model()
Load base model in float16 on CPU: AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(base_id, torch_dtype=float16, device_map='cpu')
Merge adapter with PeftModel.from_pretrained(base_model, adapter_path).merge_and_unload()
Save merged model as safetensors: merged_model.save_pretrained(output_path, safe_serialization=True)
Convert to GGUF F16: python llama.cpp/convert_hf_to_gguf.py ./merged-model --outtype f16
(Optional) Generate importance matrix for better quantization: ./llama-imatrix -m model-f16.gguf -f calibration.txt -o imatrix.dat
Quantize to target format: ./llama-quantize [--imatrix imatrix.dat] model-f16.gguf model-Q4_K_M.gguf Q4_K_M
Create Ollama Modelfile: FROM ./model-Q4_K_M.gguf with system prompt and parameters
Build and test: ollama create my-model -f Modelfile && ollama run my-model
Never re-quantize from an already quantized model; always start from F16/F32

title: vLLM: High-Throughput LLM Serving with Multi-LoRA Support
description: vLLM is the leading open-source LLM serving engine, now with Multi-LoRA inference support for MoE and dense models. vLLM 0.15.0+ achieves 454% OTPS improvement and 87% lower TTFT for LoRA-enabled MoE serving. The Production Stack provides Kubernetes-native deployment with LoRA Controller, request routing, observability, and runtime adapter loading/unloading. Supports OpenAI-compatible API, Anthropic API, and sleep mode for GPU hibernation.

title: Ollama Custom Model Import and Modelfile Reference
description: Official Ollama documentation for importing fine-tuned models via Modelfile. Covers importing safetensors models, GGUF models, LoRA adapters (safetensors and GGUF), quantization during import, setting system prompts and parameters, and sharing models via the Ollama registry. Supported architectures include Llama 2/3/3.1/3.2, Mistral 1/2/Mixtral, and Gemma 1/2. Compatible with HuggingFace, Unsloth, and MLX training outputs.

title: Deploy Fine-Tuned Model to vLLM Production Stack
description: Step-by-step workflow for deploying fine-tuned models to production using the vLLM Production Stack on Kubernetes. Covers Helm-based deployment, LoRA adapter lifecycle management, request routing, observability with Prometheus/Grafana, and runtime adapter loading/unloading. Enables serving N fine-tuned variants from a single base model on one GPU.
workflow: Install vLLM Production Stack: helm repo add vllm https://vllm-project.github.io/production-stack
Create values.yaml with base model, GPU resources, and LoRA adapter configuration
Define LoRA adapters in values.yaml under loraAdapters (local path, HuggingFace repo, or S3 path)
Deploy with: helm install vllm vllm/vllm-stack -f values.yaml
Enable runtime LoRA updating: set VLLM_ALLOW_RUNTIME_LORA_UPDATING=True
Verify deployment: kubectl get pods and check vLLM logs
Test inference: curl http://vllm-service:8000/v1/chat/completions with OpenAI-compatible API
Set up Prometheus monitoring: vLLM exposes metrics at /metrics endpoint
Create Grafana dashboard for tokens/sec, TTFT, GPU utilization, and request latency
Update adapters at runtime without restart by pushing new adapter configs

title: The Ultimate LLM Fine-Tuning Guide (PromptInjection)
description: Comprehensive guide covering the entire pipeline from dataset to GGUF deployment. Includes ms-swift framework, LoRA vs. full fine-tuning decision guide, Flash Attention, quantization options, parameter explanations (learning rate, epochs, warmup ratio, batch size, gradient accumulation), scaling guide by model size, and conversion to GGUF for local inference with LM Studio and Ollama.

title: On-Policy Preference Data Generation for DPO
description: Generate preference data from the SFT model itself rather than using external LLMs. Prompt the SFT model, score outputs with a rule-based or model-based reward function, and create (chosen, rejected) pairs where higher-scoring outputs are 'chosen'. This on-policy approach produces data that closely matches the model's distribution, leading to more effective DPO training than using off-policy data from a different model.

title: Chat Template Consistency Between Training and Inference
description: The most common silent failure in fine-tuning: using a different chat template during inference than during training. Always use tokenizer.apply_chat_template() consistently for both training data preparation and inference. Mismatched templates (e.g., Alpaca format during training but ChatML during inference) cause the model to produce garbled or unhelpful outputs despite appearing to train well.

title: Avoiding Catastrophic Forgetting in Domain Fine-Tuning
description: When fine-tuning on domain-specific data, models often lose general capability (catastrophic forgetting). Mitigation strategies include: (1) Mix 5-10% general instruction data with domain data, (2) Use a lower learning rate (2e-5 instead of 2e-4), (3) Monitor a general benchmark like MMLU alongside task metrics, (4) Use LoRA/QLoRA instead of full fine-tuning to limit parameter changes, (5) Apply early stopping when general benchmarks start degrading.

title: Quality Over Quantity in Fine-Tuning Datasets
description: The LIMA paper demonstrated that 1,000 curated examples can match 52,000 noisy ones. Production fine-tuning consistently shows that 200-500 high-quality, carefully curated examples outperform 2,000+ auto-scraped examples. Dedication of effort to data quality (accuracy, consistency, formatting, PII redaction) provides more improvement than increasing data volume. For SFT, start with 500-1,000 examples and iterate based on evaluation results.

title: PoliLegalLM: Domain-Specific LLM for Legal Reasoning
description: Research paper demonstrating a multi-stage training pipeline (continued pretraining, progressive SFT, preference-based RL) for legal domain LLMs. Outperforms models of similar scale and remains competitive with much larger models on LawBench, LexEval, and PoliLegal benchmarks. Demonstrates that well-structured multi-stage training achieves domain parity with models 10-100x larger.

title: Multi-Stage Training Pipeline for Domain Adaptation
description: Nearly all top-performing domain-specific LLMs use a multi-stage pipeline rather than single SFT: (1) Continued pretraining on domain corpus to learn domain vocabulary and knowledge, (2) Supervised fine-tuning on domain instruction data, (3) Preference alignment (DPO/RLHF) to shape output behavior. Each stage builds on the previous, and skipping stages (e.g., going directly to SFT without pretraining) leads to significantly worse results on domain benchmarks.

title: Continuous Fine-Tuning with Catastrophic Forgetting Mitigation
description: Multi-stage continual fine-tuning requires conflict resolution between new knowledge and existing capabilities. The NAACL 2025 paper introduces preference-based learning bias to identify knowledge conflicts, self-distillation data augmentation for expanding training data, and dynamic data selection using mutual information. After 5 stages of fine-tuning on Llama3-8B, this method achieves 74.8% accuracy vs. 27.7% for traditional continual instruction fine-tuning (46.9% improvement). Key metrics: Knowledge Gain Ratio, post-injection accuracy, and Knowledge Retention Rate.

title: A/B Testing Fine-Tuned Models in Production
description: Production A/B testing validates LLM improvements with real-world metrics. Meta's RLPF study achieved 6.7% CTR increase (p=0.0296) in a 10-week A/B test with 35K advertisers. Key practices: (1) Use log-binomial regression with covariates for statistical rigor, (2) Offline reward model scores correlate 0.95 Pearson with live A/B outcomes, enabling pre-deployment screening, (3) Watch for reward hacking (model gaming the metric), (4) Use multi-objective optimization (helpfulness + safety + target metric), (5) Moderate optimization levels often outperform aggressive ones.

title: HuggingFace PEFT: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Library
description: The foundational library for parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods including LoRA, QLoRA, AdaLoRA, IA3, prefix tuning, prompt tuning, and DoRA. Provides get_peft_model() for wrapping any HuggingFace model, merge_and_unload() for adapter merging, add_weighted_adapter() for multi-adapter composition, and push_to_hub() for sharing adapters. The standard integration point for Unsloth, Axolotl, and torchtune.

title: Deploying a Fine-Tuned Model with Ollama
description: Working code for deploying a fine-tuned LoRA model with Ollama. Covers merging the adapter, converting to GGUF, creating the Modelfile with system prompt and parameters, building the model, and running inference. Also shows how to share the model via the Ollama registry.
code:
# Step 1: Merge LoRA adapter into base model (Python)
from peft import PeftModel
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
import torch

base_id = 'meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct'
adapter_path = './sft-adapter'
merged_path = './merged-model'

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(base_id)
base_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    base_id, torch_dtype=torch.float16, device_map='cpu'
)
model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base_model, adapter_path)
model = model.merge_and_unload()
model.save_pretrained(merged_path, safe_serialization=True)
tokenizer.save_pretrained(merged_path)

# Step 2: Convert to GGUF (shell)
# python llama.cpp/convert_hf_to_gguf.py ./merged-model --outtype f16
# ./llama.cpp/llama-quantize ./merged-model/ggml-model-f16.gguf model-Q4_K_M.gguf Q4_K_M

# Step 3: Create Modelfile
cat > Modelfile << 'EOF'
FROM ./model-Q4_K_M.gguf

PARAMETER temperature 0.3
PARAMETER num_ctx 4096
PARAMETER repeat_penalty 1.1

SYSTEM """
You are a specialized assistant for medical coding.
Always respond with accurate ICD-10 codes and explanations.
"""
EOF

# Step 4: Build and run with Ollama
# ollama create medical-coder -f Modelfile
# ollama run medical-coder

# Step 5: Test inference
# ollama run medical-coder 'What is the ICD-10 code for Type 2 diabetes?'

# Step 6: Share via registry (optional)
# ollama cp medical-coder username/medical-coder
# ollama push username/medical-coder

title: Full Fine-Tuning to Production Deployment Pipeline
description: End-to-end workflow covering the complete lifecycle from data preparation through training, evaluation, merging, quantization, and deployment. This is the master reference workflow that connects all the sub-workflows into a single production pipeline.
workflow: Curate dataset: 500-5000 high-quality examples in chat/instruction format, deduplicated, PII redacted, 5-10% safety examples
Split dataset 90/5/5 (train/val/test), ensuring no source leakage between splits
Configure training: LoRA (r=16, alpha=32, target all-linear) or QLoRA (nf4, bf16) for 7B+ models
Train with SFTTrainer: lr=2e-5 (QLoRA) or 2e-4 (LoRA), bf16=True, gradient_checkpointing=True, packing=True
Evaluate base model on benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K, task-specific) as baseline
Evaluate fine-tuned model on same benchmarks; compare against baseline to detect catastrophic forgetting
If alignment needed: generate on-policy preference data and run DPO (beta=0.1, lr=5e-6)
Merge adapter: PeftModel.merge_and_unload() to create standalone model
Convert to GGUF and quantize (Q4_K_M for deployment, Q8_0 for testing)
Deploy: vLLM for production API serving, Ollama for local/edge, or both via Kubernetes Production Stack

title: Target All Linear Projections for LoRA (Not Just Q and V)
description: Modern best practice for LoRA fine-tuning is to target all linear projections (q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj, gate_proj, up_proj, down_proj) rather than just Q and V attention projections. Targeting all 7 layers consistently outperforms targeting only Q+V, especially for domain adaptation tasks. The overhead is minimal since LoRA parameters are tiny compared to full model weights. Set target_modules='all-linear' in PEFT for automatic detection.

title: Docker-Based Fine-Tuning Environment Setup
description: Step-by-step workflow for setting up a reproducible fine-tuning environment using Docker. Avoids CUDA version conflicts, Triton compilation failures, and transitive dependency issues that plague bare-metal setups. Uses Axolotl's pre-built Docker image as the recommended starting point, with pinned tags for reproducibility.
workflow: Pull Axolotl Docker image: docker pull winglian/axolotl:main-latest
Create working directory on host: mkdir -p ~/axolotl-projects/{data,configs,output}
Launch container with GPU access: docker run --gpus all --shm-size 16g -v ~/axolotl-projects:/workspace -it winglian/axolotl:main-latest bash
Verify environment: python -c 'import torch; print(torch.cuda.is_available(), torch.version.cuda)'
Verify key packages: pip list | grep -E 'transformers|peft|trl|bitsandbytes|flash-attn'
Create Axolotl YAML config (80-120 lines for production runs)
Launch training: accelerate launch -m axolotl.cli train config.yml
Monitor with WandB dashboard in browser
Export results: merge adapter, convert to GGUF, push to HuggingFace Hub
Pin Docker image tag (e.g., winglian/axolotl:main-2025-01-15) for reproducibility

title: ORPO Alignment Fine-Tuning Workflow
description: Step-by-step workflow for aligning an SFT model using ORPO (Odds Ratio Preference Optimization), which eliminates the need for a separate alignment stage and reference model. ORPO combines SFT and preference alignment into a single training run by adding an odds ratio penalty to the standard NLL loss, reducing training time and VRAM by ~40% compared to DPO.
workflow: Prepare preference dataset with 'prompt', 'chosen', and 'rejected' columns (same format as DPO)
Configure ORPOTrainer with beta=0.1 (odds ratio weight), learning_rate=5e-6, and QLoRA for memory efficiency
ORPO combines SFT loss (NLL on chosen) + alignment loss (log-odds ratio) in one pass: no separate reference model needed
Train with ORPOTrainer, monitoring loss/sft, loss/orpo, and log_odds_chosen/rejected metrics
Compare with DPO baseline: ORPO trains 40% faster (single stage vs. two stages) and uses ~40% less VRAM (no reference model)
Evaluate on both preference accuracy and general benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K) to check for alignment tax
Merge ORPO adapter into base model for deployment

title: DeepSpeed: Distributed Training with ZeRO Offloading
description: Microsoft DeepSpeed provides ZeRO (Zero Redness Optimizer) stages 1-3 for distributed LLM training. ZeRO-3 offloads optimizer states, gradients, and parameters across GPUs, enabling training of models up to 1 trillion parameters. Features include activation checkpointing, pipeline parallelism, MoE support, and 1-bit Adam/1-bit LAMB optimizers for 5x communication reduction. DeepSpeed is integrated into Axolotl and HuggingFace Accelerate.

title: DeepSpeed ZeRO-3 QLoRA Fine-Tuning Configuration
description: Working configuration for multi-GPU QLoRA fine-tuning with DeepSpeed ZeRO-3. Demonstrates ZeRO-3 config JSON for offloading optimizer states and gradients to CPU, HuggingFace Accelerate integration, and launch commands for multi-node training. Enables fine-tuning 70B models on 2x A100-80GB GPUs with CPU offloading.
code:
# deepspeed_config.json for ZeRO-3 with CPU offloading
"""
{
  "bf16": {
    "enabled": true
  },
  "zero_optimization": {
    "stage": 3,
    "offload_param": {
      "device": "cpu",
      "pin_memory": true
    },
    "offload_optimizer": {
      "device": "cpu",
      "pin_memory": true
    },
    "overlap_comm": true,
    "contiguous_gradients": true,
    "sub_group_size": 1e9,
    "reduce_bucket_size": "auto",
    "stage3_prefetch_bucket_size": "auto",
    "stage3_param_persistence_threshold": "auto",
    "stage3_max_live_parameters": 1e9,
    "stage3_max_reuse_distance": 1e9,
    "stage3_gather_16bit_weights_on_model_save": true
  },
  "gradient_accumulation_steps": "auto",
  "gradient_clipping": "auto",
  "train_batch_size": "auto",
  "train_micro_batch_size_per_gpu": "auto"
}
"""

# Launch multi-GPU training
# accelerate launch --use_deepspeed --config_file deepspeed_config.json \
#   --num_processes 4 train.py

# Or with torchrun:
# torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 train.py --deepspeed deepspeed_config.json

# Fine-tune 70B model on 2x A100-80GB with CPU offloading:
# torchrun --nproc_per_node=2 train.py \
#   --model_name meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct \
#   --deepspeed deepspeed_config.json \
#   --batch_size 1 --gradient_accumulation_steps 16

title: mergekit: Model Merging for Fine-Tuned LLMs
description: mergekit is the standard tool for merging multiple fine-tuned LLMs into a single model. Supports TIES (Truncated Importance Election), DARE (Drop And Rescale), SLERP (Spherical Linear Interpolation), linear averaging, and passthrough merging. Enables composing specialized adapters (e.g., code + math + multilingual) into one model without additional training. Supports LoRA merging, FrankenLora layer stitching, and GPU-accelerated merging via CuPy.

title: Model Merging with mergekit: TIES and SLERP
description: Working YAML configurations and commands for merging multiple fine-tuned models using mergekit. Demonstrates TIES merging for combining domain-specific experts, SLERP for smooth interpolation between two models, and weighted linear merging. Shows how to compose a code-expert + math-expert model into a single model that retains both capabilities.
code:
# TIES merge configuration (merges 3 models, uses one as base)
cat > ties_merge.yaml << 'EOF'
models:
  - model: meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
    # base model (no parameters needed)
  - model: my-org/llama3-code-finetuned
    parameters:
      density: 0.5  # keep top 50% of changes
      weight: 0.5
  - model: my-org/llama3-math-finetuned
    parameters:
      density: 0.5
      weight: 0.5
merge_method: ties
base_model: meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
dtype: bfloat16
EOF

# SLERP merge (smooth interpolation between 2 models)
cat > slerp_merge.yaml << 'EOF'
models:
  - model: meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
  - model: my-org/llama3-code-finetuned
merge_method: slerp
base_model: meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
dtype: bfloat16
parameters:
  t: 0.5  # interpolation factor (0.0 = base, 1.0 = finetuned)
EOF

# Run merge (install: pip install mergekit)
# mergekit-yaml ties_merge.yaml ./merged-ties-model
# mergekit-yaml slerp_merge.yaml ./merged-slerp-model

# Merge LoRA adapters directly (no base model loading)
# mergekit-yaml lora_merge.yaml ./merged-lora

title: GRPO Fine-Tuning Workflow (DeepSeek-R1 Style)
description: Step-by-step workflow for fine-tuning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), the algorithm behind DeepSeek-R1's reasoning capabilities. GRPO eliminates the need for a critic model by using group-relative rewards, reducing VRAM by ~25% vs. PPO. Each prompt generates a group of completions, ranked against each other, and the model learns to prefer higher-ranked outputs.
workflow: Start with a strong SFT model (e.g., Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct or DeepSeek-R1-Distill)
Define a rule-based or model-based reward function for your task (e.g., format compliance, answer correctness, reasoning quality)
Configure GRPOTrainer with num_generations=8-16 (group size per prompt), beta=0.04 (KL penalty), learning_rate=1e-6
For each prompt, generate num_generations completions, score each with the reward function, and compute group-relative advantages
Train with GRPOTrainer, monitoring reward mean, reward std, and KL divergence to reference model
GRPO uses ~25% less VRAM than PPO because no critic model is needed, but requires more compute per step (multiple generations per prompt)
Evaluate on reasoning benchmarks (GPQA, MATH-500, LiveCodeBench) and general benchmarks (MMLU) to check for alignment tax
Merge the GRPO adapter for deployment; the model should now show improved chain-of-thought reasoning

title: MT-Bench and LLM-as-Judge for Fine-Tuned Model Evaluation
description: Comprehensive guide to evaluating fine-tuned LLMs using MT-Bench (multi-turn benchmark) and LLM-as-Judge methodology. MT-Bench evaluates conversation quality across 8 categories with 80 multi-turn questions. LLM-as-Judge uses GPT-4 or Claude to rate model outputs on a 1-10 scale, achieving 0.85 correlation with human annotators. Covers prompt design for judging, inter-rater agreement metrics, and common evaluation pitfalls like verbosity bias and position bias.

title: LoRA+ and QA-LoRA: Improved Fine-Tuning Methods
description: LoRA+ improves on standard LoRA by using different learning rates for the A and B matrices (eta_B = 16 * eta_A), achieving 2-5% better downstream performance at the same training cost. QA-LoRA (Quantization-Aware LoRA) introduces group-wise operators that align quantization and LoRA dimensions, eliminating the accuracy degradation that standard QLoRA can cause on large-scale models. Both are available in PEFT via use_dora=True and in Axolotl configs.

title: Multi-Task Fine-Tuning with Task-Specific Adapters
description: Step-by-step workflow for fine-tuning a single base model on multiple tasks using task-specific LoRA adapters, then composing them at inference time. Each task gets its own adapter trained independently, and PEFT's add_weighted_adapter() merges them with configurable weights. This avoids catastrophic forgetting across tasks and enables dynamic adapter routing in vLLM production serving.
workflow: Define tasks and curate separate training datasets for each (e.g., summarization, code generation, medical QA)
Fine-tune one LoRA adapter per task on the shared base model using the same LoRA config (r, alpha, target_modules)
Evaluate each task-specific adapter on its own benchmark to establish per-task baselines
Compose adapters using PEFT: model.add_weighted_adapter(['summarization', 'code', 'medical'], weights=[0.4, 0.3, 0.3], adapter_name='combined')
Evaluate the combined adapter on all task benchmarks to check for positive transfer or interference
Deploy with vLLM dynamic adapter loading: load base model once, serve N adapters from a single GPU
Implement adapter routing: classify incoming requests by task, route to the appropriate adapter
Monitor per-task latency and accuracy; if a task degrades, reduce its weight in the composition or keep it as a separate adapter

title: Flash Attention 2 for Memory-Efficient Fine-Tuning
description: Flash Attention 2 is a hardware-aware attention algorithm that reduces memory from O(N^2) to O(N) and achieves 2-4x wall-clock speedup over standard PyTorch attention. Essential for fine-tuning models with long context windows (4K-128K tokens). Integration requires attn_implementation='flash_attention_2' in from_pretrained(), torch >=2.0, and flash-attn package. Reduces peak VRAM by 30-50% for 8K+ sequence lengths, enabling larger batch sizes and longer sequences on the same GPU.

title: Reward Model Training for RLHF Pipeline
description: Step-by-step workflow for training a reward model as part of an RLHF pipeline. The reward model learns human preferences from comparison data and provides the signal for PPO-based alignment. Covers reward model architecture (base model + scalar head), preference dataset formatting, training with comparison loss, and evaluation by measuring agreement with held-out human preferences (target: >70% accuracy).
workflow: Collect preference data: pairs of (chosen, rejected) responses for the same prompt, rated by human annotators
Format as dataset with columns: prompt, chosen, rejected (same format as DPO data)
Initialize reward model: base model (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B) + classification head outputting a scalar score
Train with RewardTrainer using comparison loss: loss = -log(sigmoid(r(chosen) - r(rejected)))
Use learning_rate=1e-5, batch_size=64, and train for 1 epoch to avoid overfitting
Evaluate on held-out preference pairs: target >70% agreement with human preferences
Check for reward hacking: the model should not give high scores to obviously bad outputs
Use the trained reward model in PPO pipeline or for filtering on-policy preference data for DPO

title: Knowledge Distillation as Fine-Tuning Pretraining
description: Use knowledge distillation from a larger teacher model to generate high-quality training data for a smaller student model. The teacher generates completions for your prompts, which become the student's SFT targets. This produces domain-aligned data with teacher-level reasoning quality, at a fraction of the teacher's inference cost after distillation. Particularly effective for creating reasoning-focused SFT data when GPT-4/Claude-level outputs are needed but inference cost is a concern at scale.

title: HuggingFace AutoTrain: No-Code LLM Fine-Tuning
description: HuggingFace AutoTrain provides a web UI and CLI for fine-tuning LLMs without writing code. Supports SFT, DPO, and reward model training. Upload a CSV/JSONL dataset, select a model, configure hyperparameters (or use defaults), and launch training on free T4 GPUs or paid A100s. Outputs a merged model ready for deployment. Best for rapid prototyping and teams without ML engineering resources.

title: Iterative DPO for Continuous Model Improvement
description: Iterative DPO (also called online DPO or self-play DPO) repeatedly generates on-policy preference data from the current model, trains a new DPO adapter, merges it, and repeats. Each iteration improves the model's alignment and capability. Meta's Llama 2 paper and subsequent work show 3-5 iterations typically converge, with diminishing returns after that. Key: always generate preference data from the current (improved) model, not the original SFT model, to avoid distribution mismatch.

title: Fine-Tuning LLMs with LoRA (tutorialQ)
description: Practical LoRA fine-tuning tutorial covering rank/alpha selection, target modules, dataset preparation, training configuration, and merging. Includes working code for both HuggingFace PEFT and Unsloth.

title: LLM Fine-Tuning Complete Guide 2026 (youngju.dev)
description: Comprehensive 2026 guide covering LoRA, QLoRA, PEFT, full fine-tuning, dataset preparation (SFT, DPO, GRPO), evaluation, and deployment. Includes production-ready code examples and troubleshooting.

title: WeClone
description: One-stop solution for creating your AI twin from chat history. 17k+ stars. Fine-tunes LLMs with your chat logs to capture unique style, then binds to a chatbot.

title: H2O LLM Studio
description: Framework and no-code GUI for fine-tuning LLMs. 4.9k+ stars. Visual interface for configuring SFT, DPO, and RLHF training with experiment tracking.

title: LlamaGym
description: Fine-tune LLM agents with online reinforcement learning. 1.2k+ stars. Provides a Gym-style interface for training LLM-based agents through RL feedback loops.

title: mlx-tune
description: Fine-tune LLMs on Mac with Apple Silicon using MLX. 1.2k+ stars. Supports SFT, DPO, GRPO, Vision, TTS, STT, Embedding, and OCR fine-tuning with Unsloth-compatible API.

title: Fine-Tuning LLMs Book (dvgodoy)
description: Official repository of 'A Hands-On Guide to Fine-Tuning LLMs with PyTorch and Hugging Face' book. 825+ stars. Covers SFT, DPO, and RLHF with practical code examples.

title: Memento - Fine-tuning LLM Agents
description: Official code for Memento: fine-tuning LLM agents' policy networks without modifying the base LLM. 2.4k+ stars. Enables agent specialization at a fraction of the cost of full LLM fine-tuning.

title: MLX-GRPO
description: Pure MLX-based training pipeline for fine-tuning LLMs using GRPO on Apple Silicon. 240+ stars. Enables reinforcement learning fine-tuning on Mac hardware.

title: LLM-Zero-to-Hundred
description: Collection of LLM chatbot projects (RAG, agents) and well-known techniques for training and fine-tuning LLMs. 553+ stars. Progressive learning path from basics to advanced.
